notes: Coverage is the last 7 days in Asia/Taipei.
Items are deduplicated against the prior week by id and source URL; this week contains six new items.

highlights.title: MOHW specialist social-work training organization applications enter the final week
highlights.why: The June 29 deadline is a practical social-work capacity signal: institutions that want specialist training recognition must finish their application package now.
highlights.next: Use this as a checklist prompt for any partner-facing social-work training or supervision proposal before 2026-06-29.

highlights.title: AI-agent governance shifted from model-only safety to operating controls
highlights.why: Google DeepMind's AI Control Roadmap, OpenAI's enterprise spend controls, and Codex automation fixes all point to the same operational pattern: visibility, permissions, fallback, and cost controls matter as much as model choice.
highlights.next: Add an explicit controls row to future AI workflow pilots: who can run it, what it can access, how spend is bounded, and how failure is detected.

highlights.title: Discord continues turning quests and monthly rewards into retention infrastructure
highlights.why: Nitro Rewards/Orbs show a mature community-growth pattern that is more relevant to member engagement than generic gamification statistics.
highlights.next: Prototype one lightweight FamilyFin-style weekly challenge/reward loop on paper before building anything.

items.title: MOHW specialist social-work training organization applications close on June 29
items.oneLine: MOHW's 115-year specialist social-worker training organization recognition window for new applicants runs through 2026-06-29.
facts: MOHW's Social Assistance and Social Work Department says new applicants can apply from 2026-04-30 through 2026-06-29.
Extension applicants have a separate 2026-07-16 to 2026-09-15 window.
Applications are handled online through the social-welfare institution and professional manpower management information system.
yourTake: This is a capacity-building signal rather than a headline policy change.
For Kevin's social-work and workforce lens, it is useful because specialist training recognition shapes which institutions can credibly run supervision or continuing education pipelines.
actions: Before 2026-06-29, capture the application requirements as a reusable checklist for future social-work training partner review.
Tag any partner or institution that might need the extension window from 2026-07-16.

sources.label: MOHW Social Assistance and Social Work Department - specialist social-work training organization recognition

credibility.reason: Primary MOHW department notice with concrete dates and application route.

followups: Recheck after 2026-06-29 for any late notice, FAQ, or applicant-status update.

items.title: FSC used the bank general managers meeting to push SME finance plus carbon-check practices
items.oneLine: FSC's June 16 bank general managers meeting combined SME lending recognition with sharing on financial carbon-check services for small and micro enterprises.
facts: FSC held the 61st domestic bank general managers business liaison meeting and recognition ceremony on 2026-06-16.
The meeting recognized banks for SME lending, New Southbound target-country credit, and six core strategic industry lending.
FSC invited the Small and Medium Enterprise and Startup Administration and high-performing banks to share experience on matching SMEs into carbon-check services.
yourTake: This is relevant to financial education because SME finance is being connected to carbon-awareness and advisory workflows, not only credit volume.
For FamilyFin-style education, the reusable angle is turning abstract policy into a simple business-owner checklist.
actions: Draft one plain-language explainer outline: what an SME financial carbon check is, what documents a small business should prepare, and what questions to ask the bank.
Keep this as background material only unless a concrete audience or partner asks for it.

sources.label: FSC - 61st domestic bank general managers meeting

credibility.reason: Primary FSC news release describing the meeting and policy topics.

followups: Watch whether FSC or SME Administration publishes a reusable carbon-check guide.

items.title: Codex changelog fixed scheduled automations to honor approval mode
items.oneLine: OpenAI's Codex changelog says scheduled automations now honor the selected approval mode and also adds workspace/plugin and usage-limit guidance improvements.
facts: The Codex changelog entry includes improved plugin management across workspace plugins.
It improves usage-limit errors with inline plan and workspace guidance, including reset timing when available.
It fixes scheduled automations so they honor the selected approval mode.
yourTake: This maps directly onto Kevin's recurring automation practice: approval mode is a boundary, not just a UI setting.
The changelog reinforces keeping explicit local safety gates in prompts and scripts even when product behavior improves.
actions: For any automation with external impact, keep the approval boundary in the task prompt and not only in app settings.
Add this item to the next Codex automation governance review as evidence that approval-mode semantics are product-relevant.
followups: During the next automation audit, verify whether local automation approval settings and prompt gates still align.

items.title: OpenAI introduced usage analytics and spend controls for ChatGPT Enterprise
items.oneLine: OpenAI announced credit usage analytics and updated spend controls so enterprise admins can see adoption patterns and bound AI costs.
facts: OpenAI published the enterprise usage analytics and spend controls update on 2026-06-18.
The update is aimed at giving admins more visibility, control, and confidence in AI deployments.
The stated use case includes tracking credit usage, understanding adoption patterns, and managing costs.
yourTake: The weekly workflow lesson is that AI adoption is becoming an operations-management problem, not just a prompt-quality problem.
For Kevin's local automations, cost and usage visibility should be recorded beside quality and source checks when a workflow becomes recurring.
actions: Add a lightweight usage/cost line to future automation run summaries when a workflow becomes expensive or frequent.
Keep local artifacts as the audit trail even if provider dashboards improve.

sources.label: OpenAI - New usage analytics and updated spend controls for enterprises

followups: Watch whether similar usage-control features appear for Codex workspace automations.

items.title: Google DeepMind published an AI Control Roadmap for agent security
items.oneLine: Google DeepMind framed agent security as a multilayered control problem for increasingly capable and imperfectly aligned AI systems.
facts: Google DeepMind published the roadmap on 2026-06-18.
The post presents AI control as part of a multilayered approach to agent security.
It emphasizes integrating control protocols with broader infrastructure to manage advanced AI models safely.
yourTake: This is a useful counterweight to model-release hype because it asks what controls exist after an agent has tools, access, and delegated goals.
For Kevin's workflows, it supports the existing pattern of separating data access, approval mode, state files, and human checkpoints.
actions: Add an agent-control checklist to any future browser-backed or account-connected automation: access, monitoring, halt condition, and audit log.
Use this source when explaining why reliable automations need explicit boundaries even when the model is strong.

sources.label: Google DeepMind - Securing internal systems against increasingly capable and imperfectly aligned AI

followups: Turn this into a small internal automation-control checklist if Kevin asks to formalize the next automation review.

items.title: Discord expanded Nitro Rewards with monthly Orbs and quest multipliers
items.oneLine: Discord's Nitro Rewards update gives Nitro members monthly Orbs and quest multipliers, turning quests into a recurring reward loop.
facts: Discord says Nitro members unlock 250 Orbs every month.
The same update adds an Orbs Multiplier so completed Quests earn more Orbs.
Orbs can be redeemed in the Shop for profile items and other benefits.
yourTake: The useful pattern is not the specific reward currency. It is a repeatable loop: monthly grant, action multiplier, visible redemption, and profile/status payoff.
For member education or community operations, this suggests starting with small recurring challenges and visible progress instead of a heavy points system.
actions: Sketch one weekly member challenge with a non-monetary reward/status signal and a simple completion proof.
Avoid building a full loyalty platform until one manual loop produces evidence of repeat participation.

credibility.reason: Primary company product post; useful as product-pattern evidence, not as independent retention-performance proof.

followups: Compare this with any Taiwan member-community behavior before adapting it to FamilyFin.
